Possibilities

Debby Couture

Founder & Consultant

Twenty years inside graduate admissions — nearly a decade on the teams at Stanford's School of Education and School of Engineering, and 12 years since as an independent consultant. My clients range from first-generation undergraduates to mid-career professionals switching fields, from international applicants to parents navigating a process that can feel completely opaque. What I bring to every engagement is an insider's read on what committees are actually looking for, and the experience to help each person present their most compelling, authentic case.

Alongside that work, I founded College Minds — a podcast and coaching practice focused on mental health and wellbeing in college. It grew from my own experience as a first-generation student: the emotional weight, the imposter syndrome, the not knowing where to ask for help. After 20 years watching students face the same things, I knew there was more to do. When AI tools started reshaping what was possible, I leaned in — building automations, research tools, and workflows that let me serve clients more deeply without losing the human judgment that makes this work matter. This portfolio is what I've built so far.

If you're a prospective grad student, a researcher, or someone working on access and equity in higher education, I'd love to connect. You can also book a session with me directly on Leland.

My current focus

What I'm building now

⚡

AI Workflow Automation

Building automations that handle the repeating parts of my work: email triage, content pipelines, and scheduled tasks. So the time I spend is on decisions, not execution.

💻

Productivity Systems

Designing personal operating systems that keep both practices running smoothly, from client intake to content scheduling to AI-assisted research workflows.

🧣

Building and Documenting in Public

Everything I build gets documented here: what I made, how I made it, and what I would do differently. A running record of learning in public.

🎓

20 Years of Knowledge, Now On-Demand

I am turning two decades of admissions expertise, handouts, and frameworks into an intelligent knowledge base clients can tap into anytime. Faster answers, deeper guidance, and the full weight of my experience available well beyond our sessions together.

🎙

College Minds Content Pipeline

The podcast content exists. The work now is distribution: using AI to get the podcast, resources, and coaching offer out to the students who need it.

🌍

Coaching Client Growth System

A pipeline that converts College Minds listeners into coaching clients through email, messaging, and outreach, so the business catches up to the mission.

AI projects

What I've built with AI

Each project is something I built using AI tools, from design systems and skills to automations and research workflows. Cards with a blue top border are fully documented.

Featured Presentation

Peer-reviewed research on why college students in distress don't seek support — and what mental health coaches can do to reach them.

I built this presentation to answer a question College Minds kept raising for me. Why do students who clearly need coaching never reach out? The research points to something specific. Students in the moderate stress range don't see themselves as struggling enough to warrant help. That one finding shaped the outreach language in my Coaching Client Growth System and changed how I position coaching across my Social Media Content pipeline. For any coach building a client base with college students, this is the data that changes how you talk about what you do.

They're Stressed. They're Not Coming In.

9 slides  ·  Use ← → keys or the buttons to navigate

AI Builder Course

What I learned in 6 sessions

I came in thinking AI was a tool. Six sessions later, I think of it as infrastructure.

The inventory: two design system skills, a voice guide trained on 40 real sent emails, an email manager that drafts in my exact register, a social content pipeline, a podcast automation that writes and posts every Monday without me, a 4-page PDF worksheet, an admissions knowledge base, research and data analysis builds, a file organization script, a full College Minds practice kit, and a system auditor that flags when things drift. Sixteen things that exist and run.

One thing I want to be clear about: all the content and wording is mine. The voice, the point of view, the way I talk to my audience. AI helps surface and connect recent research to what I'm already saying, so I'm not starting from scratch every week. But the ideas are mine.

The shift happened somewhere around session three, when I stopped describing what I wanted and started specifying it: audience, output format, file path, tone register. That's the actual skill. The tools matter less than the clarity.

My strongest build is the Weekly Brief. It runs. I don't touch it. That's what I was aiming for.

What's next: threading College Minds end-to-end. The brand skill is built, the design system exists, the content pipeline is running. The gap is connecting them so a brief becomes a post becomes a site update without me in the middle.

Built with AI  ·  wisegradadmissions.com  ·  collegemindspodcast.com

1

Found an inspiration website

I opened the WISE Graduate Admissions site and asked Claude to help me understand what made it look and feel the way it does, before extracting a single color code.

2

Used Claude in Chrome to extract the design DNA

Claude browsed the live site and pulled out the exact colors, font names, layout structure, button styles, and spacing. Plus the principles behind every choice, not just the surface specs.

3

Used /skill-creator to turn it into a reusable skill

Packaged everything into a single skill file that Claude reads before creating any branded output. No re-explaining the palette on every project.

4

Now it works everywhere

Type /wisegradadmissions-design and every build (websites, documents, presentations) automatically matches the brand. This portfolio is built with it.

This popup is a live example of the design system it documents: colors, fonts, spacing, and principles all applied directly.

Color Palette

Cobalt Blue #2177F3 CTA buttons, accents
Electric Blue #116DFF Links, hover states
Deep Espresso #1D0E03 Primary text, headings
Warm Taupe #564A42 Body / secondary text
Warm White #FAF9F5 Page backgrounds
Pure White #FFFFFF Cards, surfaces
Rich Black #141413 Footer, dark sections

Typography

Heading / Jost Light
Jost 300 ~40px / 54px Sub. Avenir LT 35-Light Never bold
Body text / DM Sans Regular. Open, friendly, legible at 14px with 30px line-height.
DM Sans 400 14px / 30px Sub. Proxima Nova

Layout Principles

  • Warmth over sterility: off-whites and brown-toned text instead of pure black on white
  • Blue as a signal, not wallpaper: reserved exclusively for interactive / action elements
  • Light typography: weight 300-400 for headings; never bold, never compressed
  • Generous whitespace: let sections breathe; don't fill every pixel
  • Square corners: no rounded buttons or inputs; crisp, modern, unfussy

Brand Vibe

Trustworthy Warm Clear Approachable Professional
1

Started with a simple scaffolded HTML page

Asked Claude to build a single-file HTML portfolio with placeholder sections (hero, about, work, projects, contact) and interview me to fill in any missing content.

2

Applied my design system skill to style it

Ran /wisegradadmissions-design to replace the default styling with my brand's exact colors, fonts, spacing rules, and layout principles. One pass, no manual tweaking.

3

Claude interviewed me to fill in real content

Instead of writing everything myself, I answered a few quick questions (name, title, focus areas, contact info) and Claude wrote the about section and work cards from my answers.

4

Built out project cards as I completed each AI Builder sprint

Each sprint I finish gets its own card with a "How I Built This" and "Documentation" popup, so the portfolio grows alongside the actual work.

Techniques Used

  • Single-file HTML: all HTML, CSS, and JavaScript in one self-contained file; nothing to install, no build step, opens anywhere
  • CSS custom properties: design system tokens stored as variables so the whole site updates when one value changes
  • CSS Grid with auto-fill: responsive card layouts that reflow on any screen width without a single media query
  • JS data array for cards: project cards rendered from a plain object, keeping content completely separate from markup
  • Pre-rendered modal templates: popup content built as hidden HTML and swapped in dynamically; no framework or dependencies needed
  • Google Fonts only: no external JS libraries, no UI frameworks; just HTML, CSS, and vanilla JavaScript
  • Design system as a second pass: structure scaffolded first, brand applied separately using a skill file

Key Prompts: Build Your Own

1. Scaffold the structure

"Build me a simple portfolio website as a single HTML page. Interview me about any info you don't have. Google Fonts are the only allowed external resource. Sections: Hero, About, Work, AI Projects, Contact."

2. Apply your brand

"Use /[your-design-skill] to restyle the portfolio. Update the existing HTML file in my working directory."

3. Build out a project card

"Build out the [Project Name] card. 'How I Built This' popup: [describe your steps]. 'Documentation' popup: [describe what to show]. Keep all other cards as Coming Soon with empty popups."

4. Iterate by section

"Update only the [section name]. Keep everything else exactly as-is."
1

Started from the Design System Skill

Instead of building from scratch, I opened the existing /wisegradadmissions-design skill and used it as the foundation. The colors, fonts, and brand vibe are inherited directly, so there's no duplication and no drift between formats.

2

Identified what's different about slides

Web pages and slide decks share a brand but have different rules. I thought through every recurring scenario: client proposals, workshop decks, school research summaries. Then I wrote specific guidance for what to always include, what to leave out, and how to handle things like data slides, quotes, and speaker notes.

3

Built three example slide structures

Abstract rules only go so far. I added three concrete layout patterns, one for client proposals, one for workshops and webinars, and one for school research summaries, so Claude has a real model to follow rather than having to invent from scratch each time.

4

Packaged it with /skill-creator

Ran the packager to turn the SKILL.md into an installable .skill file. Now I can call /wisegradadmissions-slides any time I need a branded deck, without re-explaining the brand or the layout conventions.

This skill inherits the full WISE brand identity and adds PowerPoint-specific rules. Below is a summary of what it governs.

Slide Color Rules

Warm White #FAF9F5 — default slide background
Rich Black #141413 — title, section dividers, closing CTA only
Cobalt Blue #2177F3 — left-side accent bar, key callouts, slide numbers
Warm Taupe #564A42 — body copy, captions, secondary labels

Always Include

  • Title slide (dark background)
  • Agenda / overview slide
  • Section dividers (dark background, Cobalt Blue number)
  • Closing CTA slide with Calendly link
  • Speaker notes on every slide

Always Leave Out

  • Clip art or generic stock photos
  • More than 3 bullet points per slide
  • Full sentences as bullets
  • Multiple competing accent colors
  • Drop shadows on text

Three Layout Patterns

Pattern A

Client Proposal

8–12 slides. Title, framing, About WISE, process, pricing, testimonials, CTA.

Pattern B

Workshop / Webinar

15–25 slides. Title, agenda, presenter bio, 3 content sections, takeaways, CTA.

Pattern C

School Research

5–8 slides. At a glance, strengths, fit analysis, strategy, contacts, recommendation.

1

Fed 15 existing WISE content documents into Claude

Rather than writing new content from scratch, I uploaded every guide, checklist, and worksheet already created for WISE — application timelines, school list builders, SoP guides, LOR guides, and more. Claude read all 15 files across both PDF and Word formats.

2

Claude extracted 8 content themes and wrote 24 platform-native posts

Instead of repurposing content generically, Claude identified 8 distinct themes from the material and wrote 3 versions of each — one for LinkedIn (thought-leadership tone), one for Instagram (punchy caption with hashtags), and one for Facebook (conversational storytelling). 24 posts in total, each written the way that platform actually works.

3

Generated branded Canva templates for all 3 platforms

Using the WISE brand kit connected directly in Canva, Claude generated tip/checklist template designs sized correctly for Instagram (square), Facebook (landscape), and LinkedIn (landscape). Four design candidates per platform — I picked the final three and Claude saved them directly to my Canva account.

4

Packaged everything into a ready-to-use post library

All 24 posts, with their platform labels, captions, and hashtags — are saved in a formatted Word document. Combined with the Canva templates, the output is a complete content system: drop in the caption, drop it on the template, schedule it out.

A content pipeline built entirely from existing WISE materials. No blank-page writing required — 24 posts extracted, adapted, and formatted for immediate use.

Source Documents

15 WISE guides and worksheets read and synthesised: application timelines, school list builders, SoP writing guides, LOR guides, PhD SoP guides, program decision guides, and more.

1

Created an algorithmic philosophy first

The skill doesn't start with code — it starts with a manifesto. I wrote a philosophy called "Encoded Flow": the idea that invisible expertise leaves visible traces, expressed as thousands of particles drifting along noise-driven currents until their paths accumulate into something that feels both structured and organic.

2

Implemented it in p5.js with seeded randomness

The algorithm uses layered Perlin noise to construct a flow field across the canvas. Each particle follows the field, leaving a semi-transparent trail. The same seed always produces the same composition — every variation is reproducible and shareable.

3

Tuned the palette to match the WISE brand

Colour is assigned by noise value at each particle's position: cobalt blue for high-energy regions, warm taupe for midtones, deep espresso for density. The background is the same warm white as this site. The result feels native to the brand without being designed.

4

Added live parameter controls and seed navigation

Sliders for particle count, noise scale, speed, and trail fade — all update in real time. Prev/Next/Random seed buttons let you explore infinite variations. Every state is reproducible by sharing the seed number.

A generative art system built with the algorithmic-art skill. The same algorithm produces a different composition for every seed — explore with the sliders and save any variation as a PNG.

Colour Palette

Cobalt Blue

#2177F3 — motion

Warm Taupe

#564A42 — midtones

Deep Espresso

#1D0E03 — density

Warm White

#FAF9F5 — ground

Live Parameters

  • Particle count — 500 to 4,000. More particles = denser, slower build-up.
  • Noise scale — controls how tightly the flow field curves. Low = wide sweeping arcs. High = tight turbulence.
  • Speed — how fast particles travel. Slower = finer detail. Faster = bolder strokes.
  • Trail fade — how long each trail lingers before fading. Low = ghostly. High = solid accumulation.
  • Seed — any integer. Prev/Next/Random let you browse. Every seed is reproducible.

Algorithmic Philosophy — Encoded Flow

Expertise is invisible — you can't see knowledge, only its effects. This algorithm makes that visible: thousands of particles following noise-driven currents, their accumulated trails forming dense, flowing structures that feel both purposeful and organic. Like watching thinking become form.

Skill Prompt

Create algorithmic art called "Encoded Flow".
Philosophy: invisible expertise made visible through accumulated particle traces.
Palette: cobalt blue (#2177F3), warm taupe (#564A42), deep espresso (#1D0E03) on warm white (#FAF9F5).
Use a Perlin noise flow field. Particles leave semi-transparent trails.
Include sliders for: particle count, noise scale, speed, trail fade.
Add seed navigation (prev / next / random). Make it downloadable as PNG.
1

Pulled 40 real sent emails as a corpus

Connected Claude directly to Gmail and fetched the 40 most recent sent emails. Filtered out automated replies, calendar invites, and one-liners — leaving 13 substantive threads covering every email type I actually write.

2

Analysed the corpus for voice patterns

Claude read every thread and identified sentence-level patterns, rhetorical moves, emotional register, register types (post-meeting summaries, strategic advice, feedback, check-ins, sensitive), and even punctuation habits — including that I use em-dashes, which the existing humanize-writing skill banned outright.

3

Built the voice guide with verbatim quotes

Every example in the guide is a direct quote from my real emails — not paraphrased or synthesised. The guide covers 8 sections: Core Identity, Registers, Sentence Patterns, Rhetorical Moves, Emotional Register, Do's and Don'ts, a Quick Reference table, and 6 Voice Calibration Tests.

4

Bundled it into the humanize-writing skill

The voice guide is now saved in the references/ folder of my humanize-writing skill. A mandatory rule was added: before writing or editing any email, Claude reads the guide and applies register-specific guidance on top of the existing rules. Also added an explicit em-dash override — the general ban doesn't apply to my email voice.

Built from 40 real sent emails. Every quote in this guide is verbatim. The guide lives inside the humanize-writing skill and loads automatically whenever Claude drafts or edits an email for me.

8 Sections at a Glance

1. Core Identity

Earned authority. Navigator, not support person. Gives a view; doesn't hedge into uselessness.

2. Registers

9 email types with real example quotes — from post-meeting summaries to sensitive boundary-setting.

3. Sentence Patterns

Medium sentences for analysis, short declaratives for recommendations. Heavy em-dash use — by design.

4. Rhetorical Moves

6 named moves: lead with acknowledgment, give the verdict first, name the question behind the question, and more.

5. Emotional Register

Warmth at milestones, direct empathy when things go badly, no humor, no performed casualness.

6. Do's and Don'ts

All-caps headers. Name a position. No hedged recommendations. No "I hope this email finds you well."

7. Quick Reference Table

9 email types mapped to opening move, sentence length, tone, signature moves, things to avoid, and closing style.

8. Voice Calibration Tests

6 yes/no checks: structure, tone, specificity, point of view, authenticity, readability. Run before sending any draft.

Key Voice Finding

The humanize-writing skill bans em-dashes outright — but real analysis of 40 emails showed they're a core feature of this voice, used for parenthetical elaboration throughout. The voice guide adds an explicit override: the em-dash ban does not apply to emails written in this voice.

Prompt to Activate

Draft a [email type] email to [name/context].
Apply my email voice guide in full.
Register: [post-meeting summary / strategic advice / feedback / check-in / etc.]
Key facts: [what happened / what they need to know / what action is needed]
1

Built the Email Voice Guide first

The Email Manager depends on having a precise voice model — so the voice guide came first. Claude pulled 40 sent emails, identified 9 email registers with verbatim quotes, and built a structured guide that now lives inside the humanize-writing skill.

2

Designed the triage framework

Defined what "triage" actually means for this inbox: which senders always get an immediate response, how to handle cold outreach vs. meeting requests vs. client threads, what "urgent" looks like, and which threads to skip entirely. Built this into a structured decision tree Claude follows on every run.

3

Connected Gmail and built the skill

The skill uses the Gmail MCP connector to read unread threads, classify each one, and draft replies using the voice guide. Drafts land in Gmail as drafts — ready to review and send, not auto-sent.

4

Built a summary report into every run

Each triage run ends with a structured summary: threads processed, categories (client reply, cold outreach, meeting request, FYI, action required, skip), and drafts created. Makes it easy to scan what Claude did and catch anything that needs a second look.

A Gmail triage skill that reads unread threads, classifies each one, and drafts replies in my exact voice. Drafts land in Gmail for review — nothing goes out automatically.

Triage Decision Flow

Unread thread arrives
Claude reads subject, sender, and body
Priority sender? → Draft reply immediately, mark urgent
Client thread? → Classify (advice request / scheduling / check-in / feedback) → Draft appropriate register
Meeting request? → Note it, flag for calendar review, draft brief acknowledgement
Cold outreach? → Skip or draft a one-sentence decline based on rules
Automated / no reply needed? → Log and skip

Reply Quality Rules

  • Always reads the Email Voice Guide before drafting any reply
  • Matches the register to the thread type (not one-size-fits-all)
  • Short threads get short replies — no padding
  • Uses all-caps headers only in post-meeting or strategic emails
  • Closes with a forward action, never a recap
  • Never uses filler openers ("I hope this finds you well")

Run Summary Format

TRIAGE SUMMARY — [date]
Threads processed: 12
─────────────────────
Client replies drafted: 4
Meeting requests flagged: 2
Cold outreach skipped: 3
FYI / no action: 2
Urgent (draft + flagged): 1
─────────────────────
Drafts saved to Gmail: 5

Skill Activation Prompt

Run email triage.
1. Fetch my unread Gmail threads (last 48 hours).
2. Classify each thread using the triage framework.
3. Draft replies for any thread that needs one — apply the Email Voice Guide, match the register, keep it appropriately short.
4. Save all drafts to Gmail. Do not send anything.
5. Return a triage summary.
1

Identified the gap in the listener journey

College Minds listeners were engaged but had no structured path into coaching. Mapped the gap from "casual listener" to "booked client" and landed on the missing piece: a free resource that captures email and starts a real relationship — something that felt like the show, not like a sales funnel.

2

Chose the right lead magnet

Reviewed two existing coaching worksheets and chose "If You Can See Your Way Forward" over a symptom-tracking alternative — the strengths-based framing fit the College Minds tone and had broader reach. Claude helped evaluate which would convert better and why.

3

Designed the worksheet with Python and ReportLab

Rebuilt the worksheet from scratch as a branded PDF using a Python script and ReportLab's canvas API. Custom section headers, colored accent bars, rounded writing areas, and a consistent College Minds palette. Iterated twice based on feedback before settling on the final layout.

4

Set up Mailchimp, email automation, and landing page

Configured a Mailchimp audience, uploaded the PDF to the File Manager (no attachments allowed — must link), and built a Classic Automation: an instant delivery email with the download link, followed by a personal check-in 4 days later. Created the opt-in landing page via the Mailchimp Create menu — one form, one job.

5

Built an interactive launch checklist as a live tool

Rather than handing over a static document, built an interactive 7-phase checklist as a persisted Cowork artifact — checkable steps with progress tracking, correct 2026 Mailchimp interface paths, and inline tips. A real tool I can reuse, not a one-time output.

A full-funnel system that moves College Minds podcast listeners from casual audience to coaching clients — without manual outreach at every step. Built in phases; Phase 1 (capture layer) is live.

System Architecture

1

Capture Layer

A lead magnet (downloadable guide or resource) promoted within podcast episodes. Listeners opt in via a simple landing page, joining the email list and triggering the intake quiz. Tools: landing page, email platform, Calendly.

2

Segmentation Layer

A 3–4 question intake quiz placed immediately after opt-in. Segments subscribers into: Early Researcher, Active Applicant, or Parent/Support. Each segment routes to a different email sequence. Tools: intake form, email platform tags.

3

Nurture Layer

Three 5–7 email sequences, one per segment. Emails draw from real podcast content and consulting frameworks. Sequence ends with a soft CTA: book a free 20-minute discovery call. Written by Claude using the Email Voice Skill. Tools: Claude, Email Voice Skill, email platform.

4

Warm Outreach Layer

For high-engagement subscribers (3+ opens, link clicks), Claude drafts a personalised first-touch message referencing what they interacted with. Sent manually but written at scale. Tools: Claude, Email Voice Skill, Airtable to track engagement.

5

Conversion Layer

Discovery call booking via Calendly. Post-call, AI-drafted follow-up summarises the conversation and proposes next steps in Debby’s voice. Tools: Calendly, Claude, Email Voice Skill.

Tools & Stack

College Minds Podcast Top-of-funnel traffic source — existing audience, warm and engaged
Claude + Cowork (Phase 1 build) Built the worksheet PDF, email copy, automation setup, and interactive checklist artifact
Mailchimp Hosts the audience list, runs the 2-email welcome automation, and serves the opt-in landing page
Python + ReportLab Generated the branded worksheet PDF via canvas API — custom layout, colors, and typography
Canva (connected to Mailchimp) Social graphics and email headers — synced directly to Mailchimp Content Studio
Claude + Email Voice Skill (upcoming) Will draft nurture sequences, outreach messages, and follow-ups in Debby’s exact voice

Build Order

  • Phase 1 ✓ Live: Branded lead magnet PDF + Mailchimp opt-in landing page + 2-email welcome automation + interactive launch checklist
  • Phase 2: Intake quiz + segmentation tags + 3 tailored email sequences
  • Phase 3: Engagement tracking + warm outreach templates
  • Phase 4: Calendly integration + post-call follow-up automation
    • 1

    Chose the right worksheet

    I had two existing worksheets: one symptom-tracking, one strengths-based. Claude helped me think through which would work better as a lead magnet — the one that leaves someone feeling capable, not catalogued. "If You Can See Your Way Forward" won because it ends with action steps and matches the College Minds tone.

    2

    Planned the redesign with Claude

    The original was a plain Word document — functional but not something a college student would want to open. Described the audience (young adults navigating stress and uncertainty) and the goal (approachable, warm, actionable) and used Claude to plan a layout before writing a single line of code.

    3

    Built the PDF with Python and ReportLab

    Generated the worksheet as a branded PDF using Python's ReportLab canvas API. Custom section headers with colored accent bars and number pills, warm-white writing areas with guide lines, a College Minds color palette, and the logo in the header. No design app — all code.

    4

    Iterated until it felt right

    First version had spacing issues — the title ran into the header, and horizontal lines cut across section names. Revised the Python script: added top margin breathing room, removed the separator lines, rebuilt section headers with pill backgrounds instead. Two rounds to get to something a student would actually want to fill in.

    A redesigned, branded PDF worksheet used as the lead magnet for the College Minds Coaching Client Growth System. Built entirely with Python and ReportLab — no design app required.

    Design Details

    Cobalt #2177F3 Header bar, section number pills, CTA block on final page
    Warm White #FAF9F5 Writing area backgrounds — softer than plain white, easier to read
    Orange #F47A3A Section accent color (rotates through cobalt, teal, orange, green, indigo, gold per section)
    Espresso #1D0E03 Body text — deep brown-black, warmer than pure black

    Structure

  • Page 1: Introduction & grounding — what this worksheet is and how to use it
  • Pages 2–3: Six reflection sections with writing areas and guide lines
  • Page 4: Next Steps (3 color-coded action boxes) + Notes area + cobalt CTA block

    • Technical Stack

  • Python + ReportLab canvas API — all layout drawn programmatically
  • Custom functions: draw_header(), draw_footer(), draw_section_header(), draw_question(), draw_bullet(), wrap()
  • Logo: Extracted from portfolio base64 data, saved as JPG, drawn into PDF header
  • Output: 4-page A4 PDF, hosted in Mailchimp File Manager and linked (not attached) in welcome email
    • 1

    Audited 20 years of materials

    Gathered every handout, guide, framework, and resource built up over two decades: school research templates, essay feedback rubrics, interview prep guides, program comparison sheets, and coaching notes. The raw material already existed — it just lived in scattered folders.

    2

    Organised by client journey stage

    Sorted everything into the stages a client moves through: School Selection, Application Strategy, Essay Development, Interview Prep, and Decision-Making. This structure makes the knowledge base navigable rather than overwhelming.

    3

    Fed materials into Claude as a knowledge layer

    Uploaded documents, frameworks, and annotated guides so Claude could draw on them when answering client questions. The goal: responses grounded in my actual methodology, not generic AI advice.

    4

    Built prompts that surface the right knowledge

    Wrote structured prompts for each stage of the client journey — prompts that pull from the right materials and respond in my voice. A client asking about MBA essay strategy gets my frameworks, not a generic answer.

    5

    Testing and refining with real client questions

    Running actual questions from past and current clients through the system, comparing outputs to what I would say, and closing the gaps. Each iteration makes it sharper and more distinctly mine.

    Two decades of admissions expertise made accessible on demand. This isn’t a chatbot — it’s a structured knowledge system built from real materials, real frameworks, and a real methodology developed inside Stanford and across 500+ client engagements.

    Knowledge Architecture

    1

    School Selection

    Program research frameworks, ranking methodology, fit assessment rubrics, and school comparison templates. Helps clients build a balanced, strategic list rather than chasing names.

    2

    Application Strategy

    Timeline guides, component checklists, recommender briefing templates, and positioning frameworks. The inside-admissions perspective that most applicants never get access to.

    3

    Essay Development

    Essay prompts broken down by program type, structural frameworks, feedback rubrics, and annotated examples. Covers personal statements, statements of purpose, diversity statements, and short answers.

    4

    Interview Preparation

    Question banks by program type, response frameworks, common traps, and debrief templates. Built from real interview panels and post-admit feedback gathered over 10 years at Stanford.

    5

    Decision & Transition

    Offer comparison frameworks, negotiation guides, and first-semester success resources. Because getting in is only the beginning.

    What Makes It Different

  • Source material is real: Built from actual handouts, rubrics, and frameworks used with 500+ clients — not generated from scratch by AI
  • Voice is preserved: Outputs run through the Email Voice Skill so responses sound like Debby, not a generic assistant
  • Stage-aware: Prompts are structured by where a client is in their journey, so the advice is always contextually relevant
  • Extends sessions: Clients can access guidance between appointments — reducing back-and-forth and deepening the work done in live sessions
  • Continuously updated: New materials, updated program intel, and refined frameworks added on a rolling basis

    • Tools & Stack

    Claude + Source Documents Core knowledge layer — uploaded materials ground every response in real methodology
    Email Voice Skill Ensures all outputs sound like Debby — consistent tone across every interaction
    Google Drive Source material library — organised by journey stage and kept current
    Cowork (Claude) Build environment for structuring prompts, testing outputs, and iterating the system
    A 9-slide research presentation built end-to-end with AI — from peer-reviewed literature search to ASCII-rendered slide deck. Below are three key slides from the deck.

    2 IN 3 COLLEGE STUDENTS IN DISTRESS
    SEEK NO HELP AT ALL

    They're Stressed. They're Not Coming In.

    What the research says about reaching college students who need support but don't think they do

    1 / 9

    Slide 1 — Title

    "I Don't Think I Need It" Is the #1 Barrier

    56%

    prefer to handle it alone (WHO multi-country study)

    35%

    don't know where to go, even when services exist

    Students don't think they qualify.
    They're waiting until it gets worse.
    4 / 9

    Slide 4 — Key Finding 1: Low Perceived Need

    Where to Start

    1. GET EMBEDDED, NOT ADVERTISED

    Partner with RAs, advisors, and faculty. Trusted voice referrals beat any flyer.

    2. NAME THE THING PLAINLY

    Not therapy. No diagnosis. For people who want to manage better.

    3. DESIGN FOR "I'M PROBABLY FINE"

    If your messaging assumes crisis, moderate-stress students will assume it's not for them.

    9 / 9

    Slide 9 — Recommendations

    Three tools, one workflow: research brief → presentation outline → ASCII render. The whole thing — first search to final slide — built in one session with AI doing the heavy lifting.

    How It Was Built

    1

    Research brief with self-review

    Ran parallel searches across PubMed, PMC, Frontiers, JMIR, and SAGE, filtering to peer-reviewed journals only. Compiled 20 sources into a structured markdown brief, then ran a deliberate self-review: what am I least confident about, what may be outdated, what did I miss?

    2

    Applied the humanize-writing skill to the brief

    The raw brief read like a report. The skill stripped em-dashes, trailing "-ing" editorial clauses, puffed-up significance language, and clause-stacked sentences — then rebuilt it in a direct, specific voice.

    3

    Structured as a presentation outline

    Defined the audience (mental health coaches), goal (understand the landscape, build better outreach), and constraints (max 10 slides, one idea per slide). The research was reorganized into a narrative arc: context → findings → so what → action. Each slide got a title, 2-3 bullets, and a specific visual brief — not a vague note, but something buildable.

    4

    Humanized the outline again

    A second humanize pass caught more em-dashes, a dramatic two-sentence reversal, and overly formal phrasing. Visual descriptions were upgraded from vague placeholders to specific, actionable briefs.

    5

    Rendered as an ASCII slide deck

    Each slide built as an ASCII-framed panel with a progress indicator, visual elements rendered in characters (bar charts, gradient spectrum, iceberg, two-path diagrams), and speaker notes outside each frame. One idea per slide throughout.

    Tools & Skills Used

    • WebSearch: parallel queries across PubMed, PMC, Frontiers, JMIR — peer-reviewed only, no blogs
    • Humanize-writing skill: applied twice — once to the research brief, once to the presentation outline — to remove AI tells and rebuild in Debby's voice
    • Write / Edit tools: all three deliverables saved as standalone files in the ai-builder folder
    • ASCII rendering: custom text art using Unicode box-drawing characters, block fills (░▒▓█), and diagram conventions for charts, spectrums, and path flows

    Copyable Prompt — Build Your Own

    The full research-to-presentation workflow

    Research [your topic]. Use peer-reviewed studies only — no blog posts.
For each major claim, cite a source. Compile into a research brief
as a markdown file with ~2-sentence bullet points per finding.

End the brief with a self-review section:
  - What claims are you least confident about?
  - What may be outdated?
  - What important areas did you not cover?

Apply /humanize-writing to the brief.

Then structure it as a presentation outline.
  Audience: [who they are]
  Goal: [what you want them to understand or do]
  Max 10 slides. One idea per slide. No corporate filler.
  For each slide: a short specific title, 2-3 bullets or a key
  statement, and a specific visual that would strengthen it.

Apply /humanize-writing to the outline.

Then build an ASCII rendering of the slide deck. Each slide gets
its own clearly separated section with a progress indicator.
Speaker notes go outside the frame. Keep it clean — every visual
element should serve the content, not decorate it.
    A mini one-pager built from real college data — three schools, three surprising findings, rendered as a shareable summary with stat callouts and a bar chart. Pure HTML and CSS, no libraries.

    College Data Analysis  ·  May 2026

    What the Numbers Actually Say

    Cal Poly SLO  ·  William & Mary  ·  UW Seattle — three schools, three surprises

    Cal Poly earnings

    $90,768

    Beats W&M by $17k — ranked Regional

    W&M tuition ROI

    2.8×

    vs Cal Poly's 6.7× at half the cost

    UW break-even

    2.0 yrs

    Lowest debt load despite largest campus

    10-year median earnings

    Cal Poly SLO $90,768
    UW Seattle $78,466
    William & Mary $73,490

    Source: IPEDS  ·  Bars scaled relative to Cal Poly ($90,768 = 100%)

    One dataset, one AI session, three surprising findings — turned into interactive charts and a shareable summary. Here's exactly how it was built and how you can do the same.

    How It Was Built

    1

    Loaded the dataset and asked for surprising findings

    Uploaded a 200-university Excel file covering tuition, earnings, debt, completion rates, and ROI. Asked AI to find the three most surprising findings for three specific schools — Cal Poly SLO, William & Mary, and UW Seattle — and frame them as headlines someone would click on.

    2

    Generated interactive visualizations

    From five suggested chart types, chose two: an ROI vs. tuition bubble chart (bubble size = enrollment) and a break-even timeline bar chart. Both rendered directly in the conversation using Chart.js — no separate tools or exports needed.

    3

    Styled a shareable one-pager

    Turned the top findings into a compact HTML one-pager: a dark header, three stat callouts, and a pure CSS bar chart — no JavaScript, no libraries. Designed to look polished and be embeddable anywhere.

    4

    Added to the portfolio as a documented card

    Wrote the "What I Built" preview and this documentation modal, then inserted the card into the existing portfolio using a precise targeted edit — no file rewrite, no broken styles.

    Tools & Skills Used

    • Excel file reader: AI read the .xlsx directly — all sheets, all calculated columns — without needing an export or paste
    • Chart.js (via AI): bubble chart and horizontal bar chart generated in the conversation, no separate charting tool
    • Pure HTML/CSS: the one-pager preview uses zero JavaScript or external libraries — stat callouts and bar chart built entirely with inline styles
    • Edit tool: the portfolio card was inserted with a surgical targeted edit, not a full file rewrite

    Copyable Prompt — Replicate This

    Step 1 — surface surprising findings from your dataset

    Look at everything in [your file] — the data, calculated columns,
and charts. Based on [School A], [School B], and [School C], find
the 3 most surprising findings. Give me specific numbers and school
names. Frame each finding as a headline someone would click on.

    Step 2 — build a shareable one-pager preview

    Turn these findings into a mini HTML one-pager I can embed or share.
Include: a dark header with a title and subtitle, three stat callout
boxes with the key numbers, and a simple bar chart of [metric] for
each school. Pure HTML and inline CSS only — no JavaScript, no
libraries. Make it look polished.
    A fully automated weekly content engine for College Minds. Every Monday at 8am it runs on its own — researches a college mental health topic, writes content for 6 platforms (podcast script, NotebookLM source doc, YouTube, Reddit, LinkedIn, Facebook, Substack, Wix blog), builds a branded social graphic, and delivers everything to Gmail organized by audience. No input required.

    How the automation runs

    ⏱ TRIGGER
    Every Monday at 8:00 am — runs automatically, no input required
    1

    Identify the seasonal moment

    Reads today's date and maps where college students realistically are — finals stress, post-graduation drift, back-to-school anxiety, winter slump, and so on.

    2

    Search for a research angle

    Searches for a recent academic finding (last 1–2 years preferred) from sources like APA, JED Foundation, NASPA, and journal databases — something specific and relevant to the seasonal moment.

    3

    Write the weekly brief

    Produces an 11-section markdown brief covering every platform: seasonal moment, research, coaching direction, podcast script (750-900 words for Alitu), NotebookLM source document, YouTube title and description, Reddit post, Facebook post, LinkedIn post, Substack draft, and Wix blog body. Saved as a dated .md file.

    4

    Save separate podcast and NotebookLM files

    The podcast script is saved as a clean reading copy (no clutter headers) to the podcast/ subfolder. The NotebookLM source document is saved separately to notebooklm/ — ready to upload directly to NotebookLM to generate a YouTube audio overview.

    5

    Generate a branded social graphic

    Writes a Python script and runs it to produce a 1080×1080 PNG for Facebook and LinkedIn. Brand mark, hook text, cobalt accent rule, and website URL — all within the College Minds color system. Takes a second pass to refine spacing before saving.

    6

    Push a draft blog post to Wix

    Calls the Wix Blog v3 API directly, converts the blog body into Ricos rich content format, and creates a draft post on the live College Minds site — ready to review and publish.

    7

    Deliver everything to Gmail — organized by audience

    Creates a formatted HTML email with four sections: For Students (podcast, YouTube, Reddit), For Parents (Facebook, LinkedIn, Substack), Website (Wix draft ID), and Files Saved (paths to all outputs). Cobalt top border, mobile-readable layout.

    Every Monday morning, automatically

    🎧 Podcast script .md

    750-900 words, Alitu-ready, clean reading copy

    🎥 NotebookLM source doc

    Upload to NotebookLM → YouTube audio overview

    📄 11-section brief .md

    Reddit, Facebook, LinkedIn, Substack, blog — all written

    📷 Social graphic .png

    1080×1080, branded, generated with Python

    🌎 Wix blog draft

    Posted via API, ready to publish

    ✉️ Gmail draft

    Organized by audience: students / parents / website

    A scheduled automation that runs every Monday at 8am without any input. It researches, writes content for 6 platforms, generates a branded graphic, pushes a blog draft to Wix, and delivers everything to Gmail organized by audience — connecting web search, the file system, Python, the Wix API, and Gmail in one unattended workflow.

    Tools & Connections

    Web Search Finds recent peer-reviewed research from APA, JED Foundation, NASPA, and academic journals relevant to the week's moment
    File System (ai-builder folder) Reads the voice guide on every run, writes three dated files (brief .md, podcast-script .md, notebooklm-source .md) and the social graphic .png across three content-workflow subfolders
    Python + Pillow Generates the 1080×1080 social graphic PNG in the shell sandbox — brand colors, typography at two scales, cobalt accent rule, website URL. Two-pass refinement before saving.
    Wix Blog v3 API Creates a draft post directly on the College Minds Wix site via REST API — blog body converted to Ricos rich content format
    Gmail API Creates a formatted HTML draft in the inbox — organized by audience (students / parents / website / files saved), mobile-readable, cobalt top border
    Scheduled Task (Cowork) Runs the full workflow automatically every Monday at 8am — no manual trigger needed

    How It Was Built

    1

    Designed the brief structure first

    Started by mapping out what a useful weekly brief actually contains: a seasonal moment grounded in the academic calendar, a research angle from credible sources, coaching direction with real questions to ask, and platform-ready copy for Instagram, LinkedIn, and the blog. Six sections, each with a clear job.

    2

    Connected web search to pull live research

    Rather than hardcoding topics, the automation searches for recent academic findings (last 1–2 years preferred) that match the seasonal moment. Sources include APA, JED Foundation, NASPA, and journal databases. The research angle changes every week based on what's actually current.

    3

    Built the social graphic generator with Python

    The automation writes a Python script, runs it in a shell sandbox, and saves the output PNG. Pillow renders the 1080×1080 canvas: brand colors, typography at two sizes (taupe for setup lines, espresso for landing lines), cobalt top rule, and the website URL at the bottom. Iterated twice — refined spacing on each pass. The first version was too abstract; the fix was to add an explicit "COLLEGE MINDS" brand mark, a "college coaching" label below it, and a hook line large enough to read at a glance.

    4

    Wired the Wix Blog API for zero-click publishing

    The blog body from the brief gets converted to Ricos rich content format (the Wix native structure) and posted as a draft via the Wix Blog v3 REST API. One issue to solve: curly/smart quotes inside JavaScript string literals caused parse errors — the fix was to use only straight ASCII quotes in the code. Kept the note in the workflow prompt so it doesn't recur.

    5

    Added a voice guide read on every run

    The workflow reads the humanize-writing.md guide at the start of every run before writing anything. Key rules baked in: no em-dashes, no "not just X but Y" constructions, no dramatic two-sentence reversals, contractions throughout, plain verbs. The brief sounds like Debby — not like a generated document.

    6

    Expanded to a full 6-platform content engine

    After the initial build, the workflow was extended from a 6-section brief to an 11-section package covering every platform: podcast script (750-900 words for Alitu), NotebookLM source document for YouTube, Reddit post, Facebook post (both accounts), LinkedIn, Substack draft for parents, and Wix blog body. Three separate output files now save alongside the brief. Gmail delivery is organized by audience — students, parents, website — so the content is ready to use the moment it lands.

    Copyable Prompt — Adapt This for Your Own Weekly Brief

    The core scheduled task prompt

    You are generating a weekly content brief for [Your Practice Name],
a [describe your practice] serving [your audience].

Each run:
1. Identify the seasonal moment — where is your audience right now
   emotionally and practically? Name it in one sentence.
2. Search for a recent research finding (last 1-2 years) that
   connects to this moment. Summarise in 2-3 plain sentences.
3. Write a 6-section brief: seasonal moment, research, coaching
   direction, Instagram hook + caption, LinkedIn post (ready to
   paste), blog post body.
4. Generate a branded Instagram graphic (1080x1080 PNG) using
   Python and Pillow. Brand mark at top, hook text in two sizes,
   one accent color used once only.
5. Push the blog post as a draft to [your CMS] via API.
6. Create a Gmail draft delivering the brief to [your email].

Voice rules: no em-dashes, contractions throughout, plain verbs,
specific over abstract, vary sentence length.

    Key lesson: CMS APIs need ASCII-only strings in code

    If your workflow generates JavaScript that calls a CMS API, avoid curly/smart quotes inside string literals. They look identical to straight quotes in the brief text but cause parse errors in code. Use straight ASCII quotes and apostrophes throughout any generated scripts.

    Every Monday, AI writes a complete 5-7 minute podcast script ready to record in Alitu, plus a NotebookLM source document that generates a YouTube audio overview automatically. No scripting from scratch, no production bottleneck — just upload and record.

    How the pipeline works

    ⏱ MONDAY 8AM
    Weekly Brief automation runs — podcast script and NotebookLM doc included in the output
    1

    AI writes the podcast script

    750-900 words, structured for 5-7 minutes of natural speech. Cold open, seasonal moment, research angle, coaching insight, closing prompt, and a fixed outro. Warm, direct tone — like a smart friend who knows a lot about this. Voice rules applied throughout: no em-dashes, contractions, plain verbs.

    2

    Script saved as a clean reading copy

    Saved to college-minds/content-workflow/podcast/podcast-script-[date].md — formatted for reading aloud, with natural paragraph breaks and no cluttering section headers. Open it, press record.

    3

    AI writes the NotebookLM source document

    Dense, well-organized prose covering the seasonal moment, research finding in full, 3-4 coaching insights, and 5 discussion questions structured for a conversation format. NotebookLM works best with this kind of source material.

    4

    Upload to NotebookLM → YouTube audio overview (~6 min)

    Debby uploads the saved source doc to NotebookLM. NotebookLM generates a ~6-minute conversational audio overview — this becomes the YouTube upload. One manual step, one YouTube video per week.

    5

    Record in Alitu → podcast published

    Read from the clean script file, record in Alitu. Alitu handles editing, music, and podcast feed distribution. YouTube title and description (also AI-written) are ready to paste when uploading.

    What this produces each week

    🎧 Podcast episode

    Recorded in Alitu from AI script, 5-7 min

    🎥 YouTube audio video

    ~6 min, generated by NotebookLM from source doc

    📄 Podcast script .md

    Clean reading copy, saved to podcast/ folder

    📄 NotebookLM source .md

    Upload-ready, saved to notebooklm/ folder

    A podcast and YouTube production pipeline built into the weekly content automation. The scripting is fully automated — AI writes the podcast script and the NotebookLM source document every Monday. The only manual steps are uploading to NotebookLM and recording in Alitu.

    Tools & Connections

    Claude (Weekly Brief Automation) Writes the full podcast script and NotebookLM source document as part of the Monday 8am scheduled task — no separate trigger needed
    Alitu Podcast hosting platform — Debby records from the AI script, Alitu handles editing, music, and RSS feed distribution to podcast directories
    NotebookLM Google AI tool that takes the uploaded source document and generates a ~6-minute conversational audio overview — this becomes the weekly YouTube upload
    File System (ai-builder folder) Podcast scripts saved to college-minds/content-workflow/podcast/ — NotebookLM source docs saved to college-minds/content-workflow/notebooklm/ — both dated and organized
    YouTube NotebookLM audio overview uploads directly to YouTube — title and description are AI-written in the brief, ready to paste

    How It Was Built

    1

    Identified the production bottleneck

    The podcast existed before the automation. The bottleneck was scripting: deciding what to say, how to structure it, and how long to make it. Every episode started from a blank page. The fix was to route the weekly research and seasonal moment into a full script automatically — same source material, no separate effort.

    2

    Designed the script structure for Alitu

    Alitu works best with clean, readable prose. The script structure: cold open (no "hello everyone"), name the moment, research angle in plain English, coaching insight from actual sessions, a closing question to sit with, and a fixed outro ("I'm Debby Couture. This is College Minds. See you next week."). 750-900 words targets 5-7 minutes at natural speaking pace.

    3

    Added the NotebookLM source document for YouTube

    NotebookLM generates the best audio overviews from dense, well-organized prose — not bullet points or outlines. The source document format was designed specifically for this: seasonal context up front, full research finding in paragraph form, 3-4 coaching insights written as narrative, and 5 discussion questions that give NotebookLM a conversation structure to follow. The result is a ~6-minute YouTube audio that covers the same ground as the podcast from a different angle.

    4

    Wired both files into the weekly automation

    Both outputs are now part of the Monday 8am scheduled task. The podcast script and NotebookLM source document save to dedicated subfolders alongside the main brief. The Gmail delivery includes both files' paths in the "Files Saved" section — so everything is findable and ready without hunting through folders.

    Copyable Prompt — Add a Podcast Script to Your Weekly Brief

    Podcast script section prompt

    ## Podcast Script ([Your Platform] — [length], ~[word count] words)

Write a complete, conversational podcast script.
This is [Your Name] speaking directly to a [audience] listener.
No co-host. No interview format. Just you, warmly.

Structure:
- Cold open (1-2 sentences — drop the listener into the moment)
- Name the moment (what's happening right now — specific)
- The research angle (plain language, one surprising finding)
- The coaching insight (what you see in your work, what they can do)
- A closing question or prompt to sit with this week
- Outro: "I'm [Your Name]. This is [Show Name]. See you next week."

Tone: warm, direct, a little dry. Not a pep talk. Not a lecture.

Save as: /path/to/podcast-script-[YYYY-MM-DD].md
Format for reading aloud — paragraph breaks where you'd pause.
No section headers in the reading copy.

    Key lesson: NotebookLM works best with prose, not bullets

    If you want NotebookLM to generate a good conversational audio overview, give it dense, well-written paragraphs. Bullet points and outlines produce flatter output. Write the source document as if you're briefing a smart journalist — full sentences, narrative flow, discussion questions at the end.

    337 loose files on one desktop, sorted into 10 clean folders in a single session. No manual dragging, no decisions mid-run — just a conversational brief, two clarifying questions, and a script that handled the rest.

    What the script produced

    Personal & Family 53 files

    IDs, birth certificates, passports, wills, kids' school and medical records, immigration papers

    Coaching Business 58 files

    Contracts, terms of service, coaching logs, NBHWC materials, business resources

    Client Files 62 files

    Individual client SOPs, resumes, school lists, application documents

    Grad School Resources 36 files

    General guides, templates, zip archives of application resources

    Taxes & Finance 11 files

    Tax returns, 1099s, invoices, payment records

    Photos & Images 23 files

    Photos, screenshots, graphics, logos

    Wellness & Personal 12 files

    Journals, spiritual content, ADHD workbooks

    Teaching 9 files

    Syllabi and course materials

    To Review 62 files

    Temp Office files (~$), true duplicates, old Google Drive downloads — flagged for manual review, not deleted

    Existing folders untouched

    Coaching, Pictures, Wedding Pictures, Taxes 2026, WISE Policy Information — left exactly where they were

    337

    files sorted

    10

    folders created

    Existing folders left untouched. Nothing deleted — temp files and duplicates moved to To Review for manual sorting.
    A one-session desktop cleanup that sorted 337 files into 10 folders using a conversational brief and a generated shell script. The whole thing — audit, script, run, verify — took one session.

    How It Was Built

    1

    Requested access and audited the desktop

    Asked for access to the Desktop folder, then ran a directory scan. Result: 337 loose files across dozens of types — PDFs, Word docs, JPEGs, zip archives, and Word temp files (~$ prefix). Before writing a single line of script, listed the categories out and confirmed the plan.

    2

    Asked two questions before touching anything

    Two decisions that mattered: whether to keep or remove existing folders (keep them, untouched), and what to do with temp files and duplicates (move to a "To Review" folder, don't delete). Getting those answers first meant the script could run without stopping to ask mid-way.

    3

    Wrote and ran the organisation script

    Generated a Python/bash script that created 10 destination folders and sorted each file by name pattern, extension, and content cues. Ran it, then handled the 8 edge cases that needed a second pass — files with unusual names or mixed signals on which folder they belonged in.

    4

    Verified the result

    After the script ran, pulled a folder-by-folder count to confirm everything landed correctly. 337 files across 10 folders, existing folders untouched, desktop cleared to folders only — no loose files remaining.

    Tools Used

    • Bash + Python script: generated and run in the shell sandbox — created folders, moved files by pattern, handled edge cases in a second pass
    • Directory access (request_cowork_directory): Cowork requested permission to read and write the Desktop folder before touching anything
    • AskUserQuestion: two decisions confirmed before any file was moved — existing folder handling and what to do with duplicates

    Copyable Prompt — Organise Your Own Folder

    Can you organise my [Desktop / Downloads / Documents] folder?
Before you move anything, show me the categories you're planning
to use and ask me:
1. Whether to keep or remove any existing folders
2. What to do with duplicates and temp files

Once I confirm, run the script and show me a count of what
landed in each folder when it's done.

    Key lesson: ask the two questions first — what to do with existing folders, and what to do with ambiguous files — before running the script. Answering those mid-run breaks the flow and risks inconsistent decisions.

    A full coaching practice built in one session — competitive research across all 19 Leland mental health coaches, a profile audit, rewritten copy ready to paste, a 3-tier package structure, a Notion client hub with intake and revenue tracking, a $500/month growth strategy, and a 16-slide branded event deck. Everything connected: revenue math fed the pricing, pricing shaped the profile, profile fed the deck.

    What was built, in order

    1

    Competitive research — all 19 Leland mental health coaches

    Fetched and parsed every coach profile in the category. Extracted pricing ($99–$399/hr), package structures, headline patterns, and the language appearing across 5+ coaches. Key finding: 18 of 19 coaches offer hourly only — no named programs. One named package already makes you different; three makes you categorically unique.

    2

    Live profile audit — fetched and parsed the actual Leland page

    Pulled the live profile at joinleland.com/coach/debby-couture and extracted: headline, full bio, packages, pricing, credentials, specialties, and review count. 42 reviews at 5.0 puts the profile in the top 3 for the category — the strongest asset and the least-used one.

    3

    Profile rewrite — headline, bio, and 3 named packages

    Rewrote the headline to lead with who the coaching serves rather than credentials alone. Rewrote the bio to open with the student's experience rather than a career summary, and added the College Minds Podcast as a credibility signal. Built three named packages: Discovery Session ($269), Steady Ground ($599 / 3 sessions), Full Arc ($999 / 6 sessions) — all copy ready to paste directly into Leland.

    4

    Notion client hub — intake template, session notes, revenue dashboard

    Built a complete Notion-ready markdown hub with a new-client intake questionnaire, session log table, per-session notes template, a master client tracker, and a revenue dashboard with $500/month milestone math. Includes a monthly review checklist and package quick-reference section.

    5

    Growth strategy — $500/month path with revenue math

    Built the revenue model showing exactly how many clients and sessions are needed to hit $500/month across Leland and other College Minds streams. Floor scenario: 2 hourly sessions ($538). Stable: 1 Steady Ground package ($599). Strong: 1 Full Arc ($999). Ceiling with 3–4 active package clients: $2,000–$3,500/month from Leland alone. Included a 90-day action plan with weekly milestones.

    6

    16-slide event deck — 45-minute branded presentation

    "Mental Health Coaching: What It Is and How It Can Help You." Built in pptxgenjs using the College Minds color palette — cobalt, orange, teal, green, navy — with Georgia headlines, zero bullet points, and a stat-forward layout. Includes a research-backed narrative arc, the "I'm probably fine" student framing, a coaching vs. therapy comparison, evidence slides, and a pricing CTA. QA'd by converting to images and inspecting for overlaps, contrast issues, and overflow.

    Outputs — all in one session

    📄 Strategy & Profile Playbook .docx

    Competitive research, profile audit, rewritten copy, pricing, 90-day plan

    👥 Notion Client Hub .md

    Intake template, session notes, client tracker, revenue dashboard

    📈 $500/Month Revenue Model

    Floor, stable, growing, and ceiling scenarios with milestone math

    🎨 Event Deck .pptx

    16 slides, 45 minutes, College Minds palette, zero bullet points

    A full coaching practice build completed in one session — from competitive research through a branded event deck. Six deliverables, all connected: the revenue math shaped the packaging, the packaging shaped the profile copy, the profile shaped the deck narrative.

    Tools & Connections

    • WebFetch + web search: fetched the live Leland category page and all 19 coach profiles; fetched the live Debby Couture profile page and parsed bio, packages, credentials, and review count
    • Subagents (parallel): competitive research and profile parsing ran simultaneously; strategy doc, Notion hub, and event deck also ran in parallel — total session time significantly compressed
    • docx (Node.js): generated the Strategy & Profile Playbook as a formatted Word document — US Letter, branded headings, bullet lists, pricing tables, 90-day action plan timeline
    • pptxgenjs (Node.js): built the 16-slide event deck from scratch — custom color palette, Georgia/Calibri typography, card grids, stat callouts, comparison table, no bullet points
    • LibreOffice + pdftoppm: converted the .pptx to images for visual QA — inspected all 16 slides for overlaps, overflow, and contrast issues
    • File system (Write tool): all outputs saved directly to the ai-builder/college-minds/ workspace folder
    • Memory system: saved brand palette, design feedback (no brown/espresso backgrounds), and project context for future sessions

    Key Outputs

    1

    college-minds-strategy.docx

    Six sections: competitive landscape, profile audit, ready-to-paste profile copy (headline + bio + 3 packages), pricing strategy with comparison table, revenue math, and a week-by-week 90-day action plan.

    2

    notion-client-hub.md

    Notion-ready markdown with new-client intake questionnaire, session log, per-session notes template, master client tracker table, monthly revenue dashboard, and monthly review checklist.

    3

    mental-health-coaching-event-deck.pptx

    16 slides covering the full 45-minute talk arc: the problem (2 in 3 students seek no help), what coaching is and isn't, the "I'm probably fine" student, session structure, evidence, packages, and CTA. Navy and white backgrounds, cobalt/orange/teal/green accents from the College Minds logo palette.

    Copyable Prompt — Build Your Own Practice Kit

    I want to build out my full coaching practice in one session.

About me: [your niche and background]
Platform: [Leland / other]
Current packages: [list or say "none yet"]
Income goal: $[X]/month

What I want to build:
1. Competitive research on top coaches in my category
2. Full profile copy rewrite based on what's working
3. A client hub with intake template and revenue math
4. A growth strategy with a clear path to my income goal
5. A [X]-minute event deck on [your topic]

Don't do one task at a time. Let the revenue math feed
the pricing, let the pricing feed the profile, let the
profile shape the deck. Start with the competitive
research, then move through the list in order.

    This project is still in progress. Check back soon.

    I built a skill that audits my entire AI system and proposes specific improvements. It reads every skill, file, and scheduled task in my folder, flags gaps, contradictions, and tasks still being done manually — then outputs a prioritized table of changes for review.

    Audit Output — Proposals Table

    Change Target Type Why
    Rewrite with actual triage instructions: what to look for, how to label urgency, what to draft vs. flag, output format, folder routing email-manager/SKILL.md Skill edit File was two self-referential sentences. Task had stopped running and hadn't run in a week.
    Fix the one-liner from "Threshold Stillness" to accurately describe College Minds vs. WISE brand systems MEMORY.md line 5 Memory edit The old description was the exact wrong instruction for College Minds — a trap Claude would read first.
    Create a College Minds brand skill mirroring the WISE design skill — full palette, typography, do/don't list New: college-minds-design New skill College Minds brand only lived in memory. WISE fires automatically from a skill trigger. College Minds didn't.
    Add routing rule for slide image files — archive or flag files named slide-* or slide-v* more than 2 versions old organize-ai-builder/SKILL.md Skill edit ~80 slide images from deck iterations were sitting in the college-minds root uncategorized.
    Create social-voice-guide.md covering LinkedIn, Facebook, Reddit, and Substack registers humanize-writing/references/ Skill reference Weekly brief generates 4 social formats every Monday with no format-specific voice guidance.
    Delete stale scheduled-task-update.md — earlier version of weekly brief prompt pointing at wrong file path content-workflow/scheduled-task-update.md File cleanup Creates confusion about which version is current. The SKILL.md is the live version.
    Delete verbatim copy of humanize-writing skill — already out of date, not the source of truth Claude/humanize-writing.md File cleanup The installed skill is live. This copy was already missing the email override note.

    How to Build This

    1

    Paste the prompt directly into Claude Cowork

    No skill file needed. The /learn audit runs as a direct conversation — you give Claude the prompt and point it at your folder. The output is a markdown table of proposals you review before anything changes.

    2

    Make sure your workspace folder is connected

    Claude needs access to your ai-builder folder (or equivalent). It reads every skill, scheduled task SKILL.md, file, and reference doc in the folder before generating proposals.

    3

    Review the proposals table before approving

    The audit outputs a table with: Change, Target file, Type (skill edit / memory edit / new skill / file cleanup), and Why. Nothing is applied until you explicitly say "apply all" or approve specific items.

    4

    Run it again after a few sessions of building

    The audit is most useful when your system has grown — when you have 5+ skills, scheduled tasks, and reference files that could contradict each other. Run it when things start to feel inconsistent.

    Copyable Prompt

    Paste this into Claude Cowork to run your own audit:

    Focus on auditing everything in this folder: my skills, my files, and any scheduled tasks. Tell me: - Which skills are missing instructions or would produce inconsistent output? - What's not connected that should be? - What do I keep doing manually that should be codified? Give me specific, actionable proposals. No generic advice.
